name: Denny Mt.
short_description: Steep alpine rockhounding site near North Bend offering quartz and feldspar specimens in the Cascade Mountain foothills. Challenging terrain rewards experienced collectors.
description: Denny Mt. sits at 4,104 feet elevation in the northern Cascade Range foothills of King County, offering rockhounders access to mineral-rich alpine terrain. This location is characterized by steep slopes and rugged Cascade geology, where Tertiary granitic intrusions and metamorphic rocks host quartz veins, feldspar crystals, and mica schists. The site requires solid hiking skills and proper preparation due to exposed ridge terrain and elevation gain. Collectors should expect a strenuous experience with rewarding mineral specimens found in outcropping granitic formations and talus fields on the slopes. The best finds typically occur in weathered granite exposures and along seasonal water channels where minerals concentrate naturally.
history: Denny Mt. lies within the North Cascades batholith, a massive granitic intrusion formed during the Tertiary period (65-2 million years ago) when subduction-related magmatism created the core of the modern Cascade Range. The steep slopes expose cross-sections of this ancient granite, displaying the quartz, feldspar, and mica mineral assemblages characteristic of deep crustal cooling.
tips: Search weathered granite outcrops on the upper ridges and talus slopes where frost weathering naturally exposes fresh mineral faces - quartz veins and feldspar phenocrysts stand out clearly here. Bring a magnifying lens and chisel to identify and carefully extract quality specimens from the granite matrix without excessive damage. Early morning visits before afternoon clouds roll in maximize visibility on the exposed ridge; look for clear quartz points and pink or white feldspar crystals in the granitic bedrock.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4,104 feet with steep slopes throughout the approach. Rugged alpine terrain with exposed granite outcrops, loose talus rock, and limited vegetation at higher elevations. Rocky footpath becomes increasingly steep - sturdy boots with ankle support essential. No established trail; route-finding required. Parking to prime collecting area approximately 2-3 miles hiking distance gaining 1,500+ feet elevation.
best_season: July - September. Summer provides snow-free access and stable weather for alpine hiking. July through early September offers the widest window before fall storms and early snows close high elevation approaches. Morning starts essential to avoid afternoon lightning risk on exposed ridges.
written_directions: From North Bend, take Denny Creek Road east from Highway 202 for approximately 6 miles toward the Denny Creek trailhead area. Follow Forest Service roads as they climb toward the Cascade foothills. Trailhead parking is limited - expect rough forest roads requiring high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicles for final mile approach. Establish exact current access with USFS (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest) before travel, as logging activity and road maintenance frequently alter access routes.
road_conditions: Final 1-2 miles rough forest service road with ruts, rocks, and potential seasonal washout. Passenger vehicles not recommended. High-clearance or all-wheel-drive strongly advised.
parking_notes: Limited pullout parking near Denny Creek trailhead area, capacity 4-6 vehicles. No improved facilities. Road access seasonal - verify with Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest before visiting.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) guidelines. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. National Forest regulations prohibit power tools and large-scale collecting. Respect existing trail conditions - do not widen or damage approach routes. Leave no permanent marks or excessive dig sites.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
No permanent site damage
Pack out all waste
nearest_services: North Bend, approximately 8 miles southwest. Gas, food, water, and lodging available. Last supplies should be secured before heading toward trailhead.
accessibility_notes: Steep alpine terrain with no wheelchair access. Exposed rocky slopes, loose talus, and significant elevation gain make this site unsuitable for those with limited mobility or cardiovascular limitations. Experienced hikers with proper mountain gear required. Exposed ridges create lightning hazard during afternoon thunderstorms. Not recommended for young children.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Denny Mt. near North Bend, Washington?
faq.answer: Denny Mt. primarily yields quartz crystals, feldspar specimens (often pink or white), and mica in the alpine granite outcrops. The site's Tertiary granitic composition means most finds are mineral-rich granite specimens rather than faceted gem materials. Clear quartz points and well-formed feldspar phenocrysts are the most rewarding finds for collectors.

faq.question: Is Denny Mt. rockhounding free or do I need a fee and permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Denny Mt. is free and does not require a permit. The site is on Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest public land governed by FLPMA personal use collection rules. You may collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use only at no cost.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Denny Mt., Washington?
faq.answer: The optimal rockhounding season at Denny Mt. runs July through September when alpine snow clears and weather stabilizes. July and August offer the most reliable conditions, though September provides a longer window before fall storms arrive. Avoid winter and spring when snow blocks access and loose rock presents hazards.

faq.question: How difficult is hiking to the collecting areas at Denny Mt.?
faq.answer: Denny Mt. is a hard-difficulty site suited for experienced hikers only. The 2-3 mile approach gains over 1,500 feet of elevation on steep, rough terrain with limited trail and significant loose rock. The site is not beginner-friendly and requires strong legs, proper boots, and mountaineering experience. Younger collectors should be at least 16 years old and very fit.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding at Denny Mt.?
faq.answer: Bring a geologist hammer or chisel for specimen extraction, a backpack, substantial water supply (at least 3 liters), rain gear despite summer timing, sturdy hiking boots with ankle support, and a headlamp. Leave power tools behind - National Forest rules prohibit motorized excavation. A magnifying lens helps identify quality crystals before extraction.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at Denny Mt. in King County, Washington?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are permitted on National Forest land at Denny Mt. However, the steep alpine terrain and rough footing make this site unsuitable for most dogs. Only experienced mountain dogs in excellent condition should attempt this hike. Ensure your dog has proper foot padding and bring extra water for them.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar & mica at Denny Mt. in King County. Hard alpine terrain, free public National Forest access, 25 lb limit. July-Sept best.
